name: Subitizing Flash Cards
description: Show dot arrangements briefly (1-2 seconds) and ask learners to identify the quantity without counting. Progress from arrangements of 1-4 to structured arrangements of 5-10 using familiar patterns (dice faces, ten frames). Builds instant quantity recognition and number sense.
materials: Dot cards or printed flash cards
learning_objectives: Recognize quantities up to 5 without counting
Recognize structured arrangements up to 10
Develop automatic number recognition
variations: Use finger patterns instead of dot cards
Show arrangements on a ten frame for quantities 5-10
Challenge: flash two cards and ask for the total

name: Multi-Step Word Problems Workshop
description: Present real-world scenarios requiring multiple operations to solve. Learners identify what information is given, what is asked, and plan a solution strategy. Emphasis on explaining reasoning and checking answers for reasonableness rather than speed.
learning_objectives: Identify relevant information in word problems
Select appropriate operations for each step
Verify answers for reasonableness
Explain solution strategy to peers
variations: Learners write their own multi-step problems for classmates
Remove one piece of information and ask what else is needed
Present problems with extraneous information to identify

name: Function Machine Game
description: A machine takes an input number and produces an output. Learners observe several input-output pairs, deduce the rule, then predict outputs for new inputs. Start with single operations (add 3, multiply by 2) and progress to compound rules (multiply by 2 then subtract 1). Builds algebraic reasoning and pattern recognition.
materials: Function machine cards or digital simulation
learning_objectives: Identify a rule from input-output pairs
Express rules using words and symbols
Predict outputs for new inputs
Understand functions as consistent rules
variations: Learners create their own function machines for peers to solve
Use two-step rules for advanced learners
Reverse: given the output, find the input

name: Pattern Extension Puzzles
description: Present visual patterns using shapes, colors, or numbers with missing elements. Learners identify the repeating unit, describe the rule, and extend the pattern. Progress from simple AB patterns (red-blue-red-blue) to complex patterns involving size, rotation, or numeric sequences.
learning_objectives: Identify the repeating unit in a pattern
Describe patterns using words
Extend patterns by predicting next elements
Create original patterns with clear rules
variations: Use sound patterns (clap-snap-clap-snap) for kinesthetic learners
Growing patterns where each term increases (1, 3, 6, 10...)
Find the error in a given pattern sequence

name: Tangram Challenge
description: Using the seven tangram pieces, learners create specific shapes (square, rectangle, parallelogram, animal silhouettes). Explores geometric properties, spatial reasoning, and area conservation. All seven pieces have the same total area regardless of arrangement.
learning_objectives: Compose and decompose geometric shapes
Understand that area is conserved through rearrangement
Develop spatial visualization skills
Recognize geometric properties through manipulation
variations: Use only 3-4 pieces to make simpler shapes
Create your own silhouette for others to solve
Use two tangram sets to build larger shapes

name: Symmetry Explorer
description: Investigate symmetry in shapes, letters, and natural objects. Learners fold paper shapes to find lines of symmetry, predict the result of reflecting shapes across a line, and classify objects by their number of symmetry lines. Extend to rotational symmetry for advanced learners.
learning_objectives: Identify lines of symmetry in 2D shapes
Predict the result of a reflection
Classify shapes by symmetry properties
Connect symmetry to real-world objects and art
variations: Create symmetric designs using paint and folding
Explore rotational symmetry with pattern blocks
Find symmetry in alphabet letters and classify them

name: Data Collection Survey Project
description: Learners design a survey question, collect data from at least 20 respondents, organize results into a frequency table, and create multiple representations (bar graph, pie chart, pictograph). They write a short report describing what the data shows, including measures of center.
learning_objectives: Design a clear survey question with categorical or numerical responses
Organize raw data into a frequency table
Create appropriate graphical representations
Calculate and interpret mean, median, and mode
variations: Compare results between two groups (boys vs. girls, grade 3 vs. grade 5)
Investigate whether the sample represents the larger population
Present findings to classmates and take questions

name: Probability Dice Experiment
description: Roll two dice repeatedly (at least 50 trials), record the sum each time, and build a frequency distribution. Compare observed frequencies to theoretical probabilities. Discover why 7 is the most common sum and why 2 and 12 are rare. Introduces the law of large numbers.
learning_objectives: Conduct a probability experiment with repeated trials
Record and organize experimental data
Compare experimental results to theoretical probability
Understand why some outcomes are more likely than others
variations: Use three dice and predict the most common sum
Compare results after 20, 50, and 100 trials to see convergence
Design a fair game and an unfair game using dice sums

name: Number Talks: Mental Math Strategies
description: Present a computation problem (e.g., 27 + 38) and ask learners to solve it mentally, then share strategies. Record different approaches on the board. Celebrate multiple valid methods: counting on, decomposing, compensating, using known facts. Builds computational fluency and mathematical discourse.
learning_objectives: Develop flexible mental computation strategies
Explain mathematical reasoning clearly
Recognize that problems can be solved multiple ways
Listen to and evaluate others' strategies
variations: Use subtraction, multiplication, or division problems
Present estimation challenges (Is 48 x 21 closer to 1000 or 500?)
String related problems to build on each strategy (20+30, 25+35, 27+38)

name: Equation Balance Scale Simulation
description: Use a physical or digital balance scale to model equations. Place weights on both sides to represent expressions (e.g., 2x + 3 on the left, 11 on the right). Remove equal amounts from both sides to isolate the unknown. Builds intuition for algebraic manipulation as maintaining balance.
learning_objectives: Model equations as balanced systems
Apply inverse operations to isolate unknowns
Understand that equal operations preserve equality
Translate between physical models and symbolic equations
variations: Solve equations with variables on both sides
Create your own balance puzzle for a partner
Progress to inequalities (what keeps the scale tipping one way?)

name: Area and Perimeter Optimization
description: Given a fixed length of string (perimeter), learners explore what shape encloses the maximum area. Start with rectangles on grid paper, measure areas for different dimensions with the same perimeter. Extend to irregular shapes. Discover that a circle maximizes area for a given perimeter.
learning_objectives: Distinguish between area and perimeter
Investigate the relationship between shape and enclosed area
Record and compare measurements systematically
Make and test conjectures about optimal shapes
variations: Reverse: fix the area and minimize perimeter
Explore 3D analogs (fixed surface area, maximize volume)
Connect to real-world fencing or packaging problems

name: Graph Critique and Bias Detection
description: Examine graphs from news articles, advertisements, and social media that use misleading techniques: truncated axes, cherry-picked time ranges, 3D effects that distort proportions, or missing labels. Learners identify the misleading element, explain how it distorts the message, and redraw the graph accurately.
materials: Collection of misleading graphs (printed or projected)
learning_objectives: Identify common techniques used to create misleading graphs
Explain how axis manipulation distorts data perception
Redraw misleading graphs with accurate representations
Critically evaluate data visualizations in media
variations: Create intentionally misleading graphs and challenge peers to spot the trick
Compare two graphs of the same data that tell different stories
Analyze political or commercial graphs and write a critique
